overview.summary: Arrays store same-type values in contiguous memory, making index-based access extremely fast.
Backtracking systematically explores all candidate solutions by building partial solutions incrementally and abandoning them ('pruning') as soon as they violate constraints.
Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) are the two fundamental strategies to visit every node in a graph or tree.
Binary Search finds elements in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search window.
A BST is a binary tree where every left descendant is smaller and every right descendant is larger, enabling O(log N) search, insert, and delete on average.
Dynamic Programming solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, solving each once, and storing the result to avoid redundant computation.
The fast-and-slow pointer technique (Floyd's Tortoise and Hare) uses two pointers moving at different speeds to detect cycles, find midpoints, and solve linked list problems.
Graphs are the most general data structure — they model relationships between pairs of objects using nodes (vertices) and edges.
Greedy algorithms build solutions piece by piece, always choosing the option that looks best at the current moment without reconsidering past choices.
Hash maps store data in key-value pairs, allowing lightning-fast data retrieval.
A Heap is a complete binary tree where every parent node satisfies the heap property (min or max), enabling efficient extraction of the extreme element.
Linked lists are chains of discrete nodes connected by pointers.
Prefix sums precompute cumulative totals so any range sum can be answered in O(1) time after O(N) preprocessing.
Queues are FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structures, just like a line at a store.
Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself with a smaller input, building solutions by combining results from sub-calls.
Searching algorithms find the location of a target value within a data structure. Linear search scans sequentially; binary search halves the range on sorted data.
Sliding Window turns repeated contiguous-range work into a single flowing pass across the data.
Sorting arranges elements in a specific order, creating predictability.
Stacks (LIFO) and Queues (FIFO) are the two fundamental restricted-access data structures. Understanding both and knowing when to use which is key.
Stacks are simple LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structures, like a stack of plates.
Strings are arrays of characters under the hood, but often have special immutable properties.
Trees are hierarchical data structures modeling relationships rather than flat lists.
A Trie is a tree-like data structure optimized for storing and searching strings, where each node represents a character and paths from root to leaf spell out stored words.
Two Pointers uses two moving indices to narrow the search space without nested loops.
Vectors are arrays that grow automatically when they run out of space.
